prompt: Production monitoring for a TypeScript queue worker shows growing enqueue-to-handler lag even though the exported queue backlog repeatedly falls close to zero. The captured queue samples in `incident/queue-metrics.jsonl` and handler spans in `incident/worker-spans.jsonl` are from the same interval. Diagnose the lifecycle error those artifacts reveal and repair `QueueWorker` so acknowledgement reflects completed work. A successful delivery must be acknowledged exactly once, and only after its asynchronous handler fulfills. A rejected handler must not acknowledge the delivery: it must preserve the existing release path so the job is retryable with a new attempt. The worker's live backlog telemetry and the backlog snapshots attached to spans must continue to come from the queue and must accurately include all unfinished work, both ready and currently processing; oldest backlog age must likewise include the oldest unfinished delivery. Preserve the public API, deterministic clock injection, queue ordering, attempt accounting, and span outcome reporting. Do not modify the incident evidence or protected tests. The suite must remain fully offline and deterministic.

full_distill.primary_skill: correlate queue depth and oldest-age metrics with handler span timing to identify premature acknowledgement, then restore completion-based acknowledgement without losing failed jobs
full_distill.baseline_evidence: incident spans place every acknowledgement at handler start, including a delivery whose handler later fails, while queue samples report almost no unfinished work as enqueue-to-handler lag rises; the protected suite reproduces the lifecycle error because QueueWorker acknowledges immediately after reserve
full_distill.success_contract: pending handler work remains visible in ready-plus-in-flight backlog telemetry and oldest age; fulfillment is followed by one acknowledgement; rejection follows the existing release path and permits a later attempt to succeed; span backlog snapshots and outcomes agree with those queue transitions
adversarial_factors: ready depth alone remains low while long-running in-flight work must still count as unfinished backlog
the oldest in-flight delivery predates a ready delivery, so oldest age cannot be derived only from the ready list
the handler promise remains pending while telemetry is sampled, exposing acknowledgement that happens before asynchronous completion
one captured handler fails after its acknowledgement and has no retry span
a released failure must retain its enqueue time and return with an incremented delivery attempt
